title: The Job-Switch Accelerator: Senior .NET to Lead/Architect & Big Tech
subtitle: A 15-week, gap-driven program: daily algorithms, system design under pressure, hands-on distributed .NET, cloud architecture, AI engineering, and interview execution — built for working senior engineers.
outcomes: Solve and narrate medium-level algorithm problems in Python under interview pressure, backed by a spaced-repetition system
Run a complete system design interview with capacity math, justified trade-offs, and failure-mode analysis
Build production-grade distributed .NET systems: MassTransit, RabbitMQ, Kafka, sagas, outbox, idempotent consumers
Design and deploy with gRPC, YARP, polyglot persistence, and Kubernetes
Architect Azure solutions at AZ-305 level and provision them with Terraform
Integrate AI capabilities (RAG, agents, embeddings) into .NET systems — the rare-skill differentiator
Execute the interview itself: STAR story bank, mock-loop protocol, and UAE/KSA + big-tech offer negotiation
prerequisites: 4+ years of professional software engineering (the course assumes real production experience)
Working .NET/C# knowledge — the distributed-systems modules build on it
Ability to read Python (Module 1 teaches interview Python from a C# base)
~10 hours/week of protected study time for 15 weeks
targetAudience: Senior .NET engineers targeting Lead / Solution Architect roles (especially UAE & KSA markets)
Engineers preparing for big-tech loops (Microsoft, Amazon, and comparable)
Strong individual contributors whose system design and DSA skills lag their real-world experience

sections.title: DSA Mastery in Python — The Daily Track
sections.summary: Pattern-ordered, spaced-repetition algorithm training built for working engineers: deep pattern theory, in-browser Python exercises, explained quizzes, and a graded simulated screen.

items.title: Python for the Whiteboard: Idioms, Complexity Reality & the Grind System
Code: Most Frequent Element

code.starterCode:
from collections import Counter

nums = [4, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 1, 1, 4]

# Print the most frequent element (smallest wins ties)
code.solutionCode:
from collections import Counter

nums = [4, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 1, 1, 4]
counts = Counter(nums)
best = max(counts)  # placeholder ordering
top = max(counts.values())
print(min(v for v, c in counts.items() if c == top))
code.hintMarkdown: Counter gives you counts in one line. For the tie-break, find the max count first, then take min() over values holding it.

items.title: Checkpoint: Python Internals & Complexity

questions.text: `list.pop(0)` in Python is:

options.text: O(n) — every remaining element shifts left

questions.explanation: Lists are dynamic arrays; removing the head shifts all remaining elements. Use collections.deque for O(1) at both ends.

questions.text: Which are true about Python dict/set operations? (select all)
questions.explanation: `x in list` is O(n) — a classic hidden-quadratic source. `{}` is an empty dict; an empty set is set().

questions.text: `def f(x, acc=[])` is dangerous because the default list is created once and shared across calls.
questions.explanation: Mutable default arguments persist between calls. Use acc=None and create the list inside the function.

questions.text: Building a string with `s += piece` inside a loop over n pieces is:

options.text: O(n²) — strings are immutable, each += copies

questions.explanation: Each concatenation copies the whole string so far. Collect parts in a list and "".join(parts) for O(n).

questions.text: In Python, `-7 // 2` equals:
questions.explanation: Python floor-divides toward −∞, unlike C# integer division which truncates toward 0. It matters in binary search and modular math.

items.title: Arrays, Hashing & Two Pointers: The Decision Tree
Code: Two Sum

code.starterCode:
cases = [
    ([2, 7, 11, 15], 9),
    ([3, 2, 4], 6),
    ([3, 3], 6),
]

def two_sum(nums, target):
    # return [i, j] with i < j
    pass

for nums, target in cases:
    i, j = two_sum(nums, target)
    print(i, j)
code.hintMarkdown: Store value→index AFTER checking for the complement — that is what makes case [3,3] return 0 1 instead of pairing the element with itself.

items.title: Checkpoint: Hashing & Two Pointers

questions.text: In Two Sum, you store each value in the seen-map AFTER checking for its complement so that:

options.text: Duplicate values like [3,3] target 6 resolve correctly and an element never pairs with itself

questions.explanation: Check-then-store means the current element can only match a previously seen element — which handles duplicates and the "same element twice" rule in one move.

questions.text: The canonical-form trick in Group Anagrams works because:

options.text: Equivalent inputs map to an equal key, so a dict can group them

questions.explanation: Canonicalization turns "equivalent" into "equal" — sorted("eat") == sorted("tea"). The grouping then falls out of a plain dict.

questions.text: Longest Consecutive Sequence with a set is O(n) despite nested loops because:

options.text: The inner walk only starts at sequence heads, and each element is walked at most once overall

questions.explanation: Amortized analysis: the head check (n-1 not in set) ensures each sequence is walked exactly once from its start, bounding total inner work by n.

questions.text: Which problems are prefix-sum + hashmap in disguise? (select all)
questions.explanation: Both ask "have I seen prefix − k before?" — Two Sum on running prefixes. Parentheses is a stack; 3Sum is sort + two pointers.

questions.text: Two pointers on a sorted array beats a hashmap when the priority is O(1) extra space.
questions.explanation: Hashing trades space for time; converging pointers exploit sortedness for O(1) space. Naming that trade-off aloud is the senior move.

items.title: Sliding Window & the Stack Family: Windows, Nesting, Monotonic Magic
Code: Longest Unique Substring

code.hintMarkdown: Invariant: no repeats inside the window. Expand right every step; while the new char breaks the invariant, shrink from the left. best updates after restoration.

items.title: Checkpoint: Windows & Stacks

questions.text: A variable-size sliding window is O(n) despite its inner while-loop because:

options.text: Each pointer only moves forward, so total work is bounded by 2n

questions.explanation: left never moves backward — at most n increments across the whole run. Sum of all shrinks ≤ n, so total ≤ 2n.

questions.text: In Longest Repeating Character Replacement, a stale max_freq after shrinking is safe because:

options.text: A stale max only keeps the window conservatively small — it can never report a false best

questions.explanation: The answer only improves when a genuinely higher max_freq appears. Staleness can delay growth but never inflates the recorded best — correctness holds.

questions.text: Valid Parentheses has two classic bugs. Which are they? (select all)

options.text: Popping from an empty stack on a leading closer
Forgetting to require an empty stack at the end
Using a dict for pair lookup

questions.explanation: ")(" crashes naive pops; "((" passes if you forget the final emptiness check. Edge-case discipline is what this problem actually tests.

questions.text: In Daily Temperatures, the monotonic stack stores indices rather than values because:

options.text: The answer needs distances, and indices recover values but not vice versa

questions.explanation: res[j] = i − j needs positions. From an index you can always read temps[j]; from a value you cannot recover where it was.

questions.text: Every element in a monotonic-stack pass is pushed once and popped at most once, giving O(n) total.
questions.explanation: That ≤ 2n operations bound is the amortized argument — deliver it unprompted and you have separated yourself from most candidates.

items.title: Binary Search Beyond Arrays & the Four Linked-List Mechanics
Code: Lower Bound

code.starterCode:
nums = [1, 3, 3, 5, 8, 13]
queries = [3, 4, 0, 14]

def lower_bound(nums, target):
    # index of first element >= target, or len(nums)
    pass

for t in queries:
    print(lower_bound(nums, t))
code.hintMarkdown: Half-open [lo, hi): hi starts at len(nums), not len(nums)-1. If nums[mid] >= target, mid might be the answer → hi = mid. Otherwise exclude it → lo = mid + 1.

items.title: Checkpoint: Binary Search & Linked Lists

questions.text: The half-open [lo, hi) lower-bound template cannot infinite-loop because:

options.text: Both branches strictly shrink the range: hi = mid < old hi, and lo = mid + 1 > old lo

questions.explanation: Strict shrinkage every iteration plus the invariant "answer ∈ [lo, hi)" is the whole correctness proof — say it before anyone asks.

questions.text: "Binary search on the answer" applies when:

options.text: Feasibility is monotonic over the answer space — if k works, everything beyond k works

questions.explanation: Koko, ship-packages, split-array: search candidate answers, not the data. The monotonic ok(x) predicate is the recognition trigger.

questions.text: Which are real-world instances of binary search on a monotonic predicate? (select all)

options.text: Minimum worker count to drain a queue within SLA

questions.explanation: bisect asks "is the bug present yet?"; capacity planning asks "does k workers suffice?" — both monotonic yes/no boundaries.

questions.text: In Merge K Sorted Lists with heapq, tuples need a tiebreaker index because:

options.text: On equal values the heap compares the next tuple element, and ListNode objects are not comparable — it crashes

questions.explanation: (val, i, node) — the i ensures comparison never reaches the node. A correctness detail that has sunk many otherwise-perfect solutions.

questions.text: A dummy head node removes head-mutation special cases in linked-list problems.
questions.explanation: Anchoring at dummy makes the real head an ordinary node — Remove-Nth, merges, and partitions all simplify. Using it unprompted reads as experience.

items.title: Trees & Graphs: The Round That Decides Senior Loops
Code: Number of Islands

code.hintMarkdown: Bounds-check FIRST inside sink, then mark visited by mutating to "0", then recurse 4 ways. Count goes up only when you discover an unsunk land cell in the outer scan.

items.title: Checkpoint: Trees & Graphs

questions.text: Checking only parent–child ordering fails to validate a BST because:

options.text: A grandchild can violate a grandparent’s bound while satisfying its parent — bounds must be inherited down

questions.explanation: Counterexample: root 5, right child 7, right-left grandchild 4. Locally fine, globally invalid. Pass (lo, hi) bounds — or validate via in-order sortedness.

questions.text: In level-order BFS, `for _ in range(len(q))` exists to:

options.text: Freeze the current level’s size so levels stay separated as children are enqueued

questions.explanation: Snapshot-the-size is the load-bearing trick behind Right Side View, Zigzag, and every per-level computation.

questions.text: Mark nodes visited when ENQUEUED (not dequeued) in BFS because:

options.text: Otherwise a node can enter the queue multiple times — a memory and correctness bug

questions.explanation: Two neighbors can both enqueue the same node before it is processed. Mark on enqueue to guarantee single entry.

questions.text: Which hide a graph problem? (select all)

options.text: Words differing by one letter (Word Ladder)
Finding a max in an array

questions.explanation: Cells/words/courses are nodes; adjacency/one-letter-diff/prereq pairs are edges. Recognition is the skill — traversal is the easy part.

questions.text: Kahn’s algorithm detects a cycle when nodes processed < total nodes.
questions.explanation: If a cycle exists, its members never reach indegree 0 and are never taken. "Leftovers ⇒ cycle" — the same logic resolves DI and build-order dependencies.

items.title: Heaps, Backtracking & a Working DP Procedure
Code: House Robber

code.hintMarkdown: State dp[i] = best loot from first i houses. Recurrence: max(skip → dp[i-1], rob → dp[i-2] + v[i]). Only two prior states are needed — hence two variables.

items.title: Checkpoint: Heaps, Backtracking & DP

questions.text: For the k largest elements of a stream you maintain a MIN-heap of size k because:

options.text: The root is the weakest of the current elite — each newcomer fights exactly one gatekeeper in O(log k)

questions.explanation: If the newcomer beats heap[0], replace it; otherwise discard. O(n log k) time, O(k) space — and it works on infinite streams, unlike sorting.

questions.text: `heapq.heapify(xs)` on n elements costs:
questions.explanation: Bottom-up heap construction is linear — a precision point that changes several top-k complexity answers.

questions.text: In backtracking, `res.append(path[:])` instead of `res.append(path)` matters because:

options.text: path keeps mutating — appending the reference makes every stored result the same (finally empty) list

questions.explanation: The snapshot copy is the single most common backtracking bug. choose → explore → unchoose mutates path the whole way through.

questions.text: Which statements about the 5-step DP procedure are correct? (select all)

options.text: State design asks: what minimally describes a subproblem?
Top-down memoization is legitimate DP and earns full credit at most companies
Greedy always matches DP on coin problems
Space squeeze applies when dp[i] depends only on a fixed window of prior states

questions.explanation: Greedy fails on coins {1,3,4} amount 6: greedy 4+1+1 vs optimal 3+3. Brute recursion → memoize → bottom-up is the de-risking sequence under pressure.

questions.text: Stuck on a DP problem live, your first move is to write a correct brute-force recursion.
questions.explanation: A correct exponential solution scores; a broken DP does not. Then say "overlapping subproblems — I’ll memoize" and add @functools.cache.

items.title: Capstone: The Simulated Screen (Graded)

rubric.criterion: Correctness across all three problems incl. edge cases
Pattern recognition & approach (brute force first, pattern named)
Communication: continuous narration, complexity unprompted
Code quality: idiomatic Module-1 Python
Self-assessment specificity & grind system evidence

assignment.submissionHint: Paste your three solutions (or a gist/repo link) plus the 300–500 word self-review and your tracker totals.

Roughly how many seconds are in a day (for capacity math)?

items.title: Scaling Reads: Caching, Replication & CDNs
Checkpoint: Scaling Reads: Caching, Replication & CDNs

questions.text: Cache-aside means:

options.text: The app checks the cache first, loads from DB on miss, then populates the cache
The cache writes to the DB automatically
The DB pushes changes to the cache

questions.text: Read replicas introduce which fundamental problem?
A thundering herd / cache stampede is mitigated by:

options.text: Request coalescing, jittered TTLs, or locks on rebuild

items.title: Scaling Writes: Sharding, Partitioning & Consistency
Checkpoint: Scaling Writes: Sharding, Partitioning & Consistency

questions.text: A good shard key primarily needs to:

options.text: Distribute load evenly and match the dominant access pattern

questions.text: Per CAP, during a network partition a system must choose between:
Hot partitions are typically caused by:

options.text: A skewed shard key (e.g., celebrity user, current date)

items.title: Queues, Streams & Backpressure
Checkpoint: Queues, Streams & Backpressure

questions.text: The main architectural reason to put a queue between services is to:

options.text: Decouple producers from consumers and absorb load spikes

questions.text: At-least-once delivery implies consumers must be:
A queue vs a log (Kafka) differs mainly in that a log:

options.text: Retains messages and lets multiple consumer groups replay them

rubric.criterion: Requirements & capacity estimation with explicit numbers
Architecture diagram: every component justified
Data model & API design quality
Bottleneck analysis + at least two failure scenarios handled
Trade-off narration in the write-up (what you rejected and why)

assignment.submissionHint: Submit your design doc (markdown or link) + diagram image/link. Time-box the design itself to 45 minutes before writing it up.

sections.title: Distributed Messaging in .NET
sections.summary: The #1 gap in Gulf Lead/Architect JDs: RabbitMQ + MassTransit, outbox, sagas, and Kafka — ending in a portfolio-grade event-driven pipeline.

items.title: Why Queues: RabbitMQ Fundamentals + MassTransit
Checkpoint: Why Queues: RabbitMQ Fundamentals + MassTransit

questions.text: In RabbitMQ, producers publish to ____ which route to ____:
MassTransit's main value over the raw RabbitMQ client is:

options.text: Message contracts, retries, error queues, and saga support out of the box

questions.text: A poison message that keeps failing should end up in:

options.text: An error/dead-letter queue after bounded retries

items.title: The Outbox Pattern & Exactly-Once Myths
Checkpoint: The Outbox Pattern & Exactly-Once Myths

questions.text: The outbox pattern solves which problem?

options.text: Atomically saving state AND publishing an event (dual-write problem)

questions.text: True exactly-once delivery across systems is:

options.text: Impossible in general; you build effectively-once with at-least-once + idempotency

questions.text: Idempotency on the consumer side is usually implemented with:

options.text: A processed-message ID store / natural idempotency keys

items.title: Sagas & Process Managers (MassTransit State Machines)
Checkpoint: Sagas & Process Managers (MassTransit State Machines)

questions.text: A saga replaces a distributed transaction with:

options.text: A sequence of local transactions plus compensating actions

questions.text: Choreography vs orchestration: orchestration means:

options.text: A central coordinator (state machine) drives the flow
Services react to each other's events with no coordinator

questions.text: Your ServiceOrder aggregate (Draft → Confirmed → In-Service → Completed) maps naturally to:

options.text: A saga state machine when steps span multiple services

items.title: Kafka for .NET: Partitions, Consumer Groups, Rebalancing
Checkpoint: Kafka for .NET: Partitions, Consumer Groups, Rebalancing

questions.text: Kafka guarantees ordering:
Max useful parallelism of one consumer group equals:
Choosing a partition key (e.g., OrderId) primarily controls:

options.text: Which events are ordered relative to each other

items.title: LAB: Event-Driven Order Pipeline (Portfolio Repo) (Graded)

rubric.criterion: Saga implemented with compensating actions that actually fire
Outbox pattern wired and demonstrably atomic with the DB write
Idempotent consumers (prove a duplicate delivery is harmless)
Failure demo: broker down / poison message handled and documented
README explaining the messaging topology

assignment.submissionHint: Submit the repo URL + a short demo script (commands to run the failure scenarios).

questions.text: Protobuf field numbers must never be:

It's faster than all alternatives in every case

questions.text: Document stores (Mongo/Cosmos) fit best when:

options.text: Data is read/written as aggregates with flexible schema per document
You need multi-row ACID joins everywhere

questions.text: Keeping the search index in sync with the primary DB is best done via:

questions.text: A liveness probe failing causes Kubernetes to:
A readiness probe failing causes Kubernetes to:

sections.title: AI Engineering Edge

items.title: RAG Done Properly: Chunking, Hybrid Search, Reranking

options.text: Keyword (BM25) and vector similarity, often with a reranker on top
